title: An autonomous LLM agent found real Salesforce data-exposure bugs from just a URL
summary: A security team's autonomous pentest agent surfaced guest-readable Apex methods and blind SOQL injection across live Salesforce sites, retiring the comfort of a "low exploitability" rating.
takeaways: Re-audit anything you down-rated as 'low exploitability' — an LLM agent now chains the tedious recon a human attacker used to skip.
Use SOQL bind variables, not string concatenation, and avoid 'without sharing' Apex classes unless you truly need them.
Treat guest-accessible Apex on Experience Cloud sites as an exposed API and systematically enumerate every method.

sources.title: Reco: Hacking Salesforce Sites with an LLM Agent

body:
## What happened

In a [post dated 2026-06-12](https://www.reco.ai/blog/hacking-salesforce-sites-with-an-llm-agent), Reco's security research team (author Nitay Bachrach) described an autonomous LLM-driven agent that performs end-to-end penetration testing of Salesforce Experience Cloud sites — the public-facing portals many companies build on Salesforce — given only a URL. Across two live case studies, the agent found that a guest-accessible Apex method (Apex is Salesforce's server-side language) returned full contact records and billing addresses from a single email parameter, and that a second site had blind SOQL injection (SOQL is Salesforce's query language). The agent then [validated and extracted real PII](https://www.reco.ai/blog/hacking-salesforce-sites-with-an-llm-agent) — names, emails, phone numbers — to prove each finding was exploitable, not theoretical.

## Why it matters

For years, teams triaged Salesforce findings as "low exploitability" because the recon was tedious: a human had to enumerate objects, map controller methods, and probe parameters by hand, so buried bugs stayed buried. Bachrach's argument is that [automated analysis doesn't miss things because they're buried](https://www.reco.ai/blog/hacking-salesforce-sites-with-an-llm-agent). An agent runs the boring enumeration at machine speed and never gets bored, so the cost of an attacker actually reaching a weak endpoint collapses. That retires "nobody would bother to find this" as a reason to defer a fix.

## How it works

1. **Reconnaissance.** The agent maps the attack surface by enumerating Salesforce objects, Apex controller methods, and routes through the Aura framework.
2. **Object analysis.** It inspects each object for sensitive data that should not be readable by an unauthenticated guest user.
3. **Method probing.** It fuzzes Apex methods, including testing whether a query mishandles injected quote characters.
4. **Exploit and validate.** It writes and runs code to confirm a finding returns real data, rather than stopping at a maybe.
5. **Severity self-review.** An adversarial pass rates the finding's true impact before reporting.

> The recon a human attacker used to skip is exactly the work an agent does for free.

## What broke

The [root causes were textbook](https://www.reco.ai/blog/hacking-salesforce-sites-with-an-llm-agent): broken access control, dynamic SOQL built by string concatenation instead of bind variables, `without sharing` Apex classes that bypass record-level permissions, and misconfigured ContentDocument visibility. The fixes are not new prompts but ordinary platform hygiene Bachrach calls "Salesforce Security 101": use bind variables in every SOQL query, avoid `without sharing` unless a method genuinely needs it, enforce least privilege for the guest user, and systematically audit all Experience Cloud sites now that the recon barrier is gone.

[Security](/guide/security/)
